nodes.label: Port Wrangler Project Vision
nodes.rationale: Port Wrangler evolves from a Docker-based database deployer into a unified local infrastructure assistant so a developer never needs separate tools (DBeaver, kafka-ui, Ollama UI) or manual docker run commands; the AI assistant operates the stack directly.

nodes.label: Final Architecture (React + Spring Boot + Spring AI Layer)
nodes.norm_label: final architecture (react + spring boot + spring ai layer)

nodes.label: Single PostgreSQL Instance For Everything
nodes.rationale: pgvector is added as an extension to the existing system Postgres rather than standing up a new vector store service, so there is one less thing for users to manage — zero-extra-service constraint.

nodes.label: Existing Service Layer As AI Tools (No Dual Maintenance)
nodes.rationale: DbInstanceService, KafkaService, and AiRuntimeService are exposed as Spring AI @Tool methods with no new business logic, so the AI goes through the same code paths as the UI, avoiding dual maintenance.
nodes.norm_label: existing service layer as ai tools (no dual maintenance)

nodes.label: SSE Streaming From Day One
nodes.rationale: Chat endpoint streams tokens starting in Phase 3 because getting SSE streaming infrastructure right once and reusing it forever beats retrofitting streaming later.

nodes.label: Replace ddl-auto with Flyway
nodes.rationale: ddl-auto: update is fine for development but risky long-term; switching to Flyway with a baseline migration before adding new tables is non-negotiable and demonstrates database migration discipline.

nodes.label: V4 pgvector + Chat + RAG Documents Schema
nodes.norm_label: v4 pgvector + chat + rag documents schema

nodes.label: Rename Repository jubilant-doodle to port-wrangler
nodes.rationale: jubilant-doodle is a GitHub auto-generated name; renaming signals project maturity to visitors.

nodes.label: AiRuntimeType Enum (Ollama, Docker Model Runner, LocalAI)
nodes.norm_label: airuntimetype enum (ollama, docker model runner, localai)

nodes.label: Tool Call Visibility in UI (expandable cards)
nodes.rationale: Showing users what tools the AI called is important for trust and debuggability.
nodes.norm_label: tool call visibility in ui (expandable cards)

nodes.label: Agent Safety Guardrails (confirmation + read-only mode)
nodes.rationale: Destructive tool calls (stopDatabase, deleteKafkaTopic) require a confirmation_required SSE event and user confirm/cancel before executing; a global read-only mode toggle disables all state-changing tools so the AI can answer questions without being able to modify infrastructure.

nodes.label: API Design Reference (v2 versioning, envelope, pagination, SSE format)
nodes.norm_label: api design reference (v2 versioning, envelope, pagination, sse format)

nodes.label: Frontend Architecture (Zustand + React Query split, routing)
nodes.norm_label: frontend architecture (zustand + react query split, routing)

nodes.norm_label: full ci/cd pipeline (all phases, docker-publish + native-installers)

nodes.norm_label: spring ai ollama config (lazy model beans, pull-model-strategy never)

nodes.label: app Service (Port Wrangler Spring Boot container)

nodes.label: PDF: Port Wrangler Project Vision
nodes.rationale: Same vision as the markdown roadmap: unified local infrastructure assistant replacing manual docker run, separate UI tools, and manual AI model config.

nodes.label: PDF: Key Design Principles Section
nodes.rationale: Restates the three key design principles: single Postgres instance, existing service layer as AI tools, SSE streaming from day one.

nodes.label: PDF: What Makes This Stand Out Versus Other Portfolio Projects
nodes.rationale: Differentiates the project via production engineering decisions (Flyway over ddl-auto, Testcontainers over mocks, SSE over polling, pgvector over separate vector DB), integration depth across Docker/Kafka/Ollama/Spring AI/pgvector, early Spring AI 1.0 adoption, open source discipline, and solving a real, immediately-understood developer problem.
nodes.norm_label: pdf: what makes this stand out versus other portfolio projects

nodes.label: Phase 1 — Quick wins (Compose export, Spring config)
nodes.norm_label: phase 1 — quick wins (compose export, spring config)

nodes.label: Phase 3 — Spring AI foundation + Model Comparison
nodes.norm_label: phase 3 — spring ai foundation + model comparison

nodes.label: Phase 5 — Agentic tools + MCP + confirmation loop + frontend
nodes.norm_label: phase 5 — agentic tools + mcp + confirmation loop + frontend

nodes.label: AgentChatService
nodes.rationale: Manual tool loop with internalToolExecutionEnabled(false) so the model proposes tool calls but the app decides whether to execute; read-only tools auto-run, write/destructive tools halt with a confirmation_request until approve=true; capped at MAX_TOOL_ROUNDS=6 to guard against runaway loops.

nodes.label: ModelRouter
nodes.rationale: chatModelFor returns the ChatModel interface (not a concrete class) so the manual tool loop is unit-testable with stubs; also de-duplicates the two existing client builders (clientFor/statelessClientFor).

nodes.label: AgentPage.jsx
nodes.rationale: Renders tool-call trace, confirm/cancel modal (red=destructive, amber=write), a client-side read-only toggle, and approve-resume; EventSource is always closed on done/onerror to avoid auto-reconnect re-firing the prompt.

nodes.label: agent_error SSE event
nodes.rationale: The agent SSE error event was renamed from 'error' to 'agent_error' because EventSource reserves the 'error' event name for connection failures — naming a server event 'error' collides with that reserved event.

nodes.label: AgentSafety
nodes.rationale: AgentSafety.WRITE = {deployDatabase, createKafkaTopic, pullModel} is classified but NOT YET implemented in InfrastructureTools as of this handover — the single most natural next feature since it makes the north-star 'deploy X and give me config' sentence work end-to-end.

nodes.label: Liquibase cutover (ddl-auto → validate)
nodes.rationale: Flip ddl-auto:update to validate and enable spring.liquibase; write a baseline reconcile changeset if drift exists — Liquibase changesets and Hibernate ddl-auto:update fight each other if run concurrently.

nodes.label: PendingConfirmation registry (planned)
nodes.rationale: Today approval re-runs the whole turn so read-only tools execute twice; a PendingConfirmation registry keyed by confirmationId plus POST /chat/agent/confirm/{id} would resume from saved conversation history instead.

nodes.label: DockerDeployEngine
nodes.rationale: One Spring constructor only; record boolean components must be named without an 'is' prefix (containerGpu, not isContainerGpu) or Jackson serialization gets confused.

nodes.label: Definition of Done (per phase tail)
nodes.rationale: Before merging a phase tail into feature/port-wrangler-ai: full gradle gate passes, every new Java class has at least one unit test, new REST endpoints have an SSE-async-dispatch slice test, new Liquibase changesets apply on a fresh DB, frontend renders without console errors and matches Tailwind tokens, and the tail is merged with --no-ff while master stays untouched.

nodes.label: ChatGPT Deep Research Report — Local LLM Model Manager
nodes.norm_label: chatgpt deep research report — local llm model manager

nodes.label: Container security & isolation practices
nodes.rationale: Run each model container with minimal privileges (non-root, dropped capabilities, no-new-privileges), network-isolate model containers to an internal Docker network, set CPU/memory limits to prevent runaway resource use, and prefer read-only volume mounts; stronger isolation (gVisor/Firecracker) considered out of scope.

nodes.label: Claude AI Management Tool proposal (chat transcript)
nodes.norm_label: claude ai management tool proposal (chat transcript)

nodes.label: SpringAiConfigExporter.java (proposed)
nodes.rationale: The Spring AI config exporter is framed as the feature that makes Port Wrangler genuinely useful beyond running containers — once a model is running, developers get a one-click copy of the exact Spring AI config needed to use it in their own app, delivering 'easy integration management' as the core value proposition.

nodes.label: Ollama as managed container vs. pre-existing (design decision)
nodes.rationale: Could treat Ollama exactly like PostgreSQL (deploy via Port Wrangler, fully managed) or assume users have Ollama installed natively and point Spring AI at localhost:11434; the managed-container approach fits the existing UX better and makes the tool fully self-contained.
nodes.norm_label: ollama as managed container vs. pre-existing (design decision)

nodes.label: Model pull as async pipeline (design decision)
nodes.rationale: Model pulls can take minutes for large models (70B is ~40GB); reusing the existing PipelineOrchestrator and SSE polling gives progress tracking for free instead of inventing a new mechanism.
nodes.norm_label: model pull as async pipeline (design decision)

nodes.label: DeepSeek Research — Local LLM AI Model Management with Spring AI
nodes.norm_label: deepseek research — local llm ai model management with spring ai

nodes.label: Recommendation: Ollama primary + plugin architecture
nodes.rationale: Implement Ollama as the primary engine for its superior Spring AI integration and developer-friendly nature; use a plugin architecture to support llama.cpp and vLLM as optional, user-selectable runtimes for advanced use cases requiring maximum performance or concurrency.

nodes.label: GPU acceleration via NVIDIA Container Toolkit
nodes.norm_label: gpu acceleration via nvidia container toolkit

nodes.label: Persistent model storage via Docker volumes
nodes.rationale: Without a persistent volume, pulling large models (20-70GB) on every restart would be disastrous; Docker volumes must persist the Ollama model cache, provisioned and managed by Port Wrangler on first start.
nodes.norm_label: persistent model storage via docker volumes

nodes.label: Security & isolation best practices (deepseek)
nodes.rationale: Drop all Linux capabilities, prevent privilege escalation, restrict CPU/memory/GPU resource usage, use a read-only root filesystem, and avoid mounting the Docker socket into LLM containers since a compromised container could control the host daemon.

nodes.label: Implement classified-but-missing WRITE tools (1.1)
nodes.rationale: AgentSafety.WRITE = {deployDatabase, createKafkaTopic, pullModel} is already declared but InfrastructureTools only implements read-only and destructive tools; adding these as thin wrappers over the same battle-tested paths (DbInstanceService.deploy, DockerDeployEngine exec, ModelPullStep) flows through the existing confirmation modal automatically and makes the north-star sentence 'deploy a redis and give me the Spring config' actually work.

nodes.label: Docker Compose import (3.1)
nodes.rationale: Symmetric to the existing ComposeExportService: parse a docker-compose.yml into DeploymentConfigs and run them through the pipeline; high value because it reuses everything and is also a great agent tool (importCompose).

nodes.label: Correction: Liquibase not Flyway
nodes.rationale: liquibase-core is already on the classpath and changesets V1-V4 exist, but spring.liquibase config is commented out and ddl-auto:update is active, causing schema drift between Hibernate-managed entities and V1__init.sql; do not add Flyway — finish the Liquibase cutover instead: baseline the current schema, enable spring.liquibase, flip ddl-auto to validate.

nodes.label: Correction: pin Spring AI 1.0.3 GA not 1.0.0-M5
nodes.rationale: 1.0.0-M5 is a milestone release, which directly violates the hard constraint 'Spring AI 1.0 GA only'; pin 1.0.3, the latest 1.0.x GA patch as of October 2025.
nodes.norm_label: correction: pin spring ai 1.0.3 ga not 1.0.0-m5

nodes.label: Correction: 26 DbType entries, not 14
nodes.rationale: DbType has 26 entries (databases plus Kafka, RabbitMQ, Conduktor, Grafana, Prometheus, Loki, MinIO, Keycloak, Vault, Nginx, Adminer, pgAdmin), repositioning Port Wrangler as a general local-infra deployer rather than just a DB tool.

nodes.label: Correction: 4-step deploy pipeline already built
nodes.rationale: ImagePull → ContainerCreate → ContainerStart → Finalise is already built and battle-tested (PipelineOrchestrator, PipelineRunner with @Async execution + post-commit re-fetch, a clean DeployStep SPI); new long-running work like model pulls should reuse the DeployStep SPI, not invent a parallel mechanism.

nodes.label: Correction: spring-ai-starter-mcp-client naming
nodes.rationale: GA renamed all Spring AI MCP starters to the spring-ai-starter-* prefix; use spring-ai-starter-mcp-client, not the old spring-ai-mcp-client-spring-boot-starter naming.

nodes.label: Correction: Dockerized Ollama on macOS is CPU-only
nodes.rationale: True for native Ollama on macOS but false for Dockerized Ollama — Docker Desktop on macOS runs a Linux VM with no GPU passthrough, so a containerized Ollama on a Mac runs CPU-only; detect this and warn the user, recommending native Ollama on Mac and containerized on Linux/NVIDIA.
nodes.norm_label: correction: dockerized ollama on macos is cpu-only

nodes.label: Vector store decision: pgvector CONFIRM
nodes.rationale: pgvector satisfies the zero-extra-service principle and gives transactional consistency with chat history since the message row and its embedding commit together; Chroma is a second Python service/store, Pinecone is cloud-only, and Weaviate is another container to sync — all violate zero-extra-service. PostgreSQL 16 is already provisioned by SystemDbProvisioner so pgvector is just a CREATE EXTENSION with no new container or port. pgvector's ANN quality degrades beyond ~1-10M vectors but local-dev scale (tens of thousands of vectors) makes this irrelevant.

nodes.label: LLM runtime decision: Ollama default + DMR as peer runtime
nodes.rationale: Ollama is the right default (OpenAI-compatible API, best Spring AI starter covering both chat and embeddings, broadest model library); Docker Model Runner deserves first-class second-runtime status since it stores models as OCI artifacts and is Compose-native, fitting the 'infra as managed Docker containers' worldview even better than Ollama; LocalAI is relegated to 'community runtime' due to smaller ecosystem and no first-party Spring AI starter.
nodes.norm_label: llm runtime decision: ollama default + dmr as peer runtime

nodes.label: Embedding model decision: nomic-embed-text default
nodes.rationale: 768 dims, Apache-2.0, strong quality-per-millisecond on local CPU, runs in the same Ollama already deployed; a pgvector column's dimension is fixed at table creation, so switching embedding models must be an explicit, destructive re-index operation — never a silent setting.

nodes.label: AI abstraction decision: Spring AI 1.0 GA pin 1.0.3
nodes.rationale: Spring AI 1.0 GA gives ChatClient, ChatMemory/MessageWindowChatMemory/JdbcChatMemoryRepository, the advisor chain, @Tool + ToolCallingManager, PgVectorStore, and an MCP client, all first-party; pinning 1.0.3 satisfies 'GA only' with no ambiguity, and the eventual 1.0.3 to 1.1.x bump is a small migration (mostly the advisor builder API), not a rewrite.
nodes.norm_label: ai abstraction decision: spring ai 1.0 ga pin 1.0.3

nodes.label: Token optimization — layered context assembly (open-relay pattern)
nodes.rationale: A bounded verbatim window plus a sub-linearly growing rolling summary plus fixed-k retrieval keeps total prompt tokens flat instead of climbing linearly with conversation length, matching open-relay's reported 30-35% savings at 10 turns and 70-80% at 40+ turns.
nodes.norm_label: token optimization — layered context assembly (open-relay pattern)

nodes.label: RecencyFrequencyScorer (design)
nodes.rationale: score = 0.35*semantic + 0.35*recency + 0.30*frequency; pgvector can only sort by vector distance so the pattern is over-fetch by vector (top k*4) then re-rank in Java by the composite score and take top-k.

nodes.label: Hybrid summarization trigger (turns AND tokens)
nodes.rationale: A single trigger is wrong: 'every N turns' over-summarises short chatty turns and under-summarises long ones, while 'every X tokens' can let turn count drift; use both (SUMMARY_EVERY_N_TURNS=4, VERBATIM_TOKEN_BUDGET=1500), whichever fires first, running as an @Async method after the reply is persisted so the user never waits for compression.

nodes.label: SSE via servlet MVC, not WebFlux (design decision)
nodes.rationale: The app is spring-boot-starter-web (servlet) + JPA; bolting on WebFlux+R2DBC for streaming would double the web stack and fight JPA persistence. Spring MVC can return Flux<ServerSentEvent<...>> directly from a @GetMapping producing TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE and stream over the servlet container's async I/O — the right call for a memory-conscious local app, avoiding a large risky refactor.
nodes.norm_label: sse via servlet mvc, not webflux (design decision)

nodes.label: InfrastructureTools (design)
nodes.rationale: Each @Tool method is a thin wrapper over existing services with no new business logic in the tool layer, so the agent executes the same code paths as the UI — same validation (DeploymentValidations), same pipeline, same recovery — considered the single most defensible design decision in the roadmap.

nodes.label: Advisor chain ordering (Context/RAG before ToolCalling)
nodes.rationale: ContextAssemblyAdvisor (order 100) and InfraRagAdvisor (order 200) deliberately sit before the ToolCallingAdvisor (auto-registered last) so the model sees full context when deciding whether to call a tool.

nodes.label: MemPalace integration assessment: do not embed
nodes.rationale: MemPalace is a Python process backed by ChromaDB, so embedding it as core would mean a second runtime and a second vector store, violating zero-extra-service; pgvector plus the §3 token-optimization design already covers the memory need at Port Wrangler's scale (infra ops on one machine, not a general personal-memory product with thousands of unrelated conversations). Building the memory system yourself is also a stronger interview story than gluing in a third-party memory service. It is still worth a small opt-in MCP-client hook (gated behind portwrangler.memory.mcp.enabled=false by default) since Port Wrangler can deploy MemPalace like any other managed container.
nodes.norm_label: mempalace integration assessment: do not embed

nodes.label: Phase 0 — Hardening (grounded roadmap)
nodes.rationale: Renaming the com.dbdeployer Java package to io.portwrangler is tempting but churns all ~85 files and every import; the mitigation is to rename only the product/artifact/repo name and leave the Java package alone since package names are invisible to users and the rename buys nothing but merge pain.

nodes.label: Phase 1 — Quick wins + Docker Compose export (grounded roadmap)
nodes.rationale: Kafka management was scoped out into a separate dedicated Kafka tool project; Port Wrangler still deploys Kafka but no longer manages it (topic CRUD, consumer-group lag, message browser, Schema Registry), saving roughly 3 weeks and sharpening the product thesis, spent instead on Compose export and Spring config bootstrap quick wins.
nodes.norm_label: phase 1 — quick wins + docker compose export (grounded roadmap)

nodes.label: Phase 2 — LLM Runtime Manager + Model Cookbook (grounded roadmap)
nodes.norm_label: phase 2 — llm runtime manager + model cookbook (grounded roadmap)

nodes.label: runtime/GpuDetector.java (design)
nodes.rationale: GPU detection avoids shelling out: inspects Docker via the SDK (docker.infoCmd() runtime presence for NVIDIA), probes device files for AMD (/dev/kfd, /dev/dri), reads OS/arch for Apple Silicon; never runs nvidia-smi from Java on the host, and for in-container confirmation reuses the existing safe argv-array execCapture rather than a shell.

nodes.label: Phase 3 — Spring AI foundation + Model Comparison (grounded roadmap)
nodes.norm_label: phase 3 — spring ai foundation + model comparison (grounded roadmap)

nodes.label: Log chunking strategy (severity into metadata)
nodes.rationale: Logs are not prose, so chunking splits on log-record boundaries (timestamp-prefixed lines), groups N adjacent records per chunk, and promotes severity (level=ERROR|WARN|INFO) into metadata so retrieval can boost it, with a severity_boost term ensuring ERROR/WARN chunks outrank INFO for the same semantic distance.

nodes.label: Phase 5 — Agentic assistant + safety + MCP server (grounded roadmap)
nodes.norm_label: phase 5 — agentic assistant + safety + mcp server (grounded roadmap)

nodes.label: Confirmation flow via manual tool execution (design)
nodes.rationale: The core safety design: read-only tools run automatically while write/destructive tools run only after explicit user approval, achieved via internalToolExecutionEnabled(false) so the app drives the loop instead of letting the advisor auto-execute; classification is read-only (never gated) / writes (confirm) / destructive (confirm with target echoed back).
nodes.norm_label: confirmation flow via manual tool execution (design)

nodes.label: stackSummary @Tool(returnDirect=true)
nodes.rationale: Answers 'what's my stack' in one tool call by combining dbInstanceService.listAll() with connectionStringBuilder to return a structured summary of every running instance; the UI surfaces this as a 'Copy as .env' / 'Copy as Spring config' button, reusing existing data fetching with zero new work.

nodes.label: portwrangler.agent.read-only flag
nodes.rationale: Strips all non-read ToolCallbacks so write tools simply don't exist in that execution context, rather than existing but being refused — a stronger safety boundary than a runtime check.

nodes.label: Runaway-loop guard (MAX_TOOL_ROUNDS=6)
nodes.rationale: Caps tool iterations per turn to guard against a model looping indefinitely on tool calls; surfaces 'agent exceeded tool budget' if the cap is exceeded.

nodes.label: Port Wrangler MCP server (design)
nodes.rationale: Exposes the same InfrastructureTools methods as MCP tools via @McpTool with identical definitions/validation/pipeline to the agent tools (no duplication), letting Cursor/Continue.dev/VS Code Copilot/Claude Desktop call Port Wrangler's infrastructure tools directly from inside the IDE without opening the app; read-only by default, with destructive tools exposed only via portwrangler.mcp.write-enabled=true and binding to localhost only to mitigate risk.

nodes.label: Database schema cutover plan (§7.1)
nodes.rationale: Today the schema is managed by Hibernate ddl-auto:update with Liquibase present but dormant; vector/AI tables can't be added cleanly on top of update because it fights changesets and silently re-derives columns, so Phase 0 must baseline the current live schema as V5__baseline.sql, enable Liquibase, and flip Hibernate to ddl-auto:validate before any further schema change is made a changeset.

nodes.label: vector_store table (shared, one HNSW index)
nodes.rationale: One shared vector_store table for all embeddings (chat messages, deployments, logs, kafka metadata), discriminated by metadata->>'type', is cheaper to maintain than four separate tables and matches Spring AI PgVectorStore's default table shape so the starter can own it; cross-domain isolation comes from the GIN index on metadata rather than separate tables. HNSW is chosen over IVFFlat because IVFFlat needs representative data to train its lists and would build a bad index on an empty fresh-install table, while HNSW has no training step.

nodes.label: chat_message table
nodes.rationale: chat_message intentionally duplicates content that also lives in SPRING_AI_CHAT_MEMORY: the Spring AI table is the framework-owned verbatim window, while chat_message is the retrievable corpus with scoring signals (access_count, last_seen, seq) — different layers of the context-assembly design that should not be conflated.

nodes.label: Port Wrangler — Code-Grounded Technical Roadmap (PDF export)
nodes.norm_label: port wrangler — code-grounded technical roadmap (pdf export)
